Name: jacobian_skip
Title: Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping for Training-Free Latency Reduction in Transformer Inference
Short Hypothesis: Transformer layers exhibit varying sensitivity to input perturbations, and this sensitivity can be quantified via Jacobian norms to enable training-free, latency-aware layer skipping without significant accuracy degradation.
Related Work: Existing layer-skipping frameworks (e.g., DASH, DySL-VLA) rely on task-specific heuristics or additional training, limiting their generalizability. Recent work on Jacobian-based analysis (e.g., NeuraLUT-Assemble) highlights the potential of Jacobian norms for understanding layer sensitivity. Our proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a training-free, Jacobian-guided layer-skipping framework that dynamically skips layers based on intrinsic activation properties, avoiding task-specific adaptations and additional training overhead.
Abstract: Transformer-based models have achieved remarkable success across various domains, but their high computational cost remains a barrier to deployment in latency-sensitive scenarios. We propose Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping (JGLS), a training-free framework that dynamically skips transformer layers based on their sensitivity to input perturbations, quantified via Jacobian norms. JGLS leverages the intrinsic properties of model activations to determine when layer skipping is permissible, eliminating the need for task-specific heuristics or additional training. We evaluate JGLS on multiple transformer architectures (e.g., GPT, BERT, ViT) across diverse tasks, demonstrating significant reductions in inference latency (up to 40%) with minimal accuracy degradation (<1%). Our results highlight the potential of Jacobian-based analysis for latency-aware inference optimization, offering a practical solution for deploying transformers in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Sensitivity Analysis - Compute Jacobian norms for each layer across multiple transformer architectures (GPT, BERT, ViT) and tasks (text classification, image classification). Use these norms to rank layers by sensitivity.
Experiment 2: Dynamic Skipping - Implement JGLS by skipping layers with low Jacobian norms during inference. Evaluate latency reduction and accuracy on benchmark datasets (e.g., GLUE, ImageNet).
Experiment 3: Comparison to Baselines - Compare JGLS to existing layer-skipping frameworks (e.g., DASH, DySL-VLA) in terms of latency, accuracy, and generalizability across tasks.
Experiment 4: Ablation Study - Analyze the impact of different Jacobian norm thresholds on latency and accuracy to identify optimal skipping strategies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead during inference. Mitigation: Use efficient approximations or pre-compute Jacobians offline.
Risk 2: Skipping layers with low Jacobian norms may degrade accuracy in tasks requiring fine-grained reasoning. Mitigation: Introduce task-specific skipping thresholds or hybrid strategies.
Limitation: JGLS may not generalize well to non-transformer architectures or tasks with highly dynamic input distributions.
